The argument starts quietly, which somehow makes it worse. Xichun stands by the open window, hands folded in her sleeves, posture controlled in the way the Jia children are taught to be when emotions threaten to surface. Hong Lu sits across from her, listening calmly, as if she is explaining something mundane rather than pleading.

“You can’t keep talking like that,” she says, her voice strained. “You act like none of this matters. Like you don’t matter.”

Hong Lu tilts his head, genuinely confused. “But I’m still here. Nothing bad has happened.”

“That’s not true,” Xichun snaps, turning to face him. “People have died. You’ve been hurt. You just— you don’t react. You don’t care if something happens to you, and I can’t keep watching you treat your life like it’s disposable.”

He considers her words carefully. The jade eye observes, unblinking. “I didn’t mean to trouble you,” he says gently. “If something happened to me, it wouldn’t be your fault.”

“That isn’t comforting,” she replies. “That’s terrifying.”

He frowns, trying to understand why. He has seen death before. He has watched servants disappear and rooms be cleaned as if nothing had happened. Life simply continues. So he offers what he believes is honesty. “I wouldn’t mind, you know,” he says softly. “If you killed me.”

The silence is immediate. Xichun’s face drains of color, and for a moment she looks at him as though he is a stranger. Not angry. Just wounded. “That,” she says quietly, “is the cruelest thing you could have said to me.”

He opens his mouth, but she is already turning away. She leaves without raising her voice, walking out with careful steps, as if any sudden movement might break something fragile inside her. The door closes.

Hong Lu remains seated, hands folded in his lap, waiting for the relief that usually follows honesty. It does not come. Instead, his chest tightens, an unfamiliar pressure that makes it hard to breathe. He blinks and notices the jade eye catching the light, recording dutifully. Somewhere, the elders are watching. He wonders if they find this interesting.

Xichun’s expression replays in his mind, the way her shoulders had stiffened as if struck. Understanding arrives slowly and painfully. He had meant to say that she didn’t have to protect him. What she heard was that her love would be a weapon. He curls his fingers into his palms, unsettled. He had offered his life as something meaningless, and in doing so had treated her feelings the same way.

“Oh,” he murmurs, the sound small.
